The surgeon performs a lateral dissection to separate the remaining mesentery from the sidewall, preparing for transrectal extraction. Indocyanine green (ICG) is visible in the right ureteral catheters, used for enhanced visualization despite being deemed excessive for this case.

The surgeon observes the colon growing into the wall along the track to the drain and proceeds to remove the drain. The lateral mobilization of the colon is completed, and the mesentery is medialized to prevent issues with bowel length. The surgeon begins dividing the thickened mesentery at the previously chosen proximal margin using multiple firings of the vessel sealer to prevent bleeding, creating a window through the mesentery while hugging the bowel wall.

The surgeon carefully hugs the bowel wall to avoid high ligations and preserve nerve integrity, while managing mesentery bulk for extraction. Delayed bleeding from the previously cut mesentery is controlled using bipolar cautery. The surgeon progresses towards the upper rectum, ensuring orientation and marking proximal and distal margins under white light after checking with ICG. The bowel is divided using scissors in cut mode, with cautery applied in areas prone to bleeding.

The surgeon divides the specimen into smaller pieces for extraction, positioning it at the rectal apex. Using a ring clamp, the surgeon carefully maneuvers the pieces through the rectum, ensuring no tearing or spillage occurs during the process.

The surgeon cleans up residual pieces of fat, placing them into the rectum. Two silk stay sutures are placed, one held by arm number one and another in the posterior midline, to aid in inverting the posterior lip for a sewn end-to-end anastomosis. The posterior suture is used to ensure the mucosa is inverted, while the anterior canal will invert everything anteriorly.

The surgeon uses three or six inch V-lock sutures to begin suturing in the posterior midline, moving around the left side. The suturing process is visible with each bite being taken. The surgeon then turns to start the anterior canal, ensuring not to cover the posterior midline. Suturing continues with careful bites, alternating between right and left hands, and starting another suture in the posterior midline heading towards the patient's right. A small piece of mesentery is caught in the stitch and is adjusted.

The surgeon lines up the bowel for suturing, using robotic arm number one to manipulate the bowel without an assist port. The canal is turned anteriorly over the right corner, ensuring the anastomosis is tension-free with well-vascularized edges. The bowel integrity appears good, holding the sutures securely without signs of char or cauterization.

And what's nice in these cases is despite the amount of time that it took to morselate the specimen and extract it and then to sew this anastomosis, when I'm done this part of the procedure, I am done. All I need to do is remove the needles from the belly and I like to close the right lower quadrant port because I think that that one is at risk for hernia. Then essentially you're done. So although my intuitive app tells me how slow I am with my anterior resections compared to the national average, I know that that's the console time, but the total OR time might be around the same. And so I am currently in a phase of doing a lot of these because I think it's a really nice way to do this operation. And sure enough, this was a same day discharge and I really didn't have any concerns for the patient who did really nicely.

The surgeon brings the two sutures from both corners anteriorly to meet at the anterior midline, ensuring symmetry in the anastomosis. A leak test is performed using a 19mm practice scope through the anastomosis, confirming no bubbles. The surgeon removes the needles and sponges, then uses a suture passer to close the right lower quadrant 8mm port. The view shifts towards the splenic flexure to clear some effluent.
